1111	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and to its localisations by three
1112	  comparison families. First, the maps \(\mathtt{dToCech}\) identify each term
1113	  \(D^p\) with the degree-\(p\) section group
1114	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) of
1115	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} as a localised module
1116	  (\(\mathtt{dToCech\_isLocalizedModule}\)), intertwining the differentials
1117	  (\(\mathtt{cechCoface\_dToCech}\), \(\mathtt{dToCech\_comm}\)); the section-side
1118	  cofaces are \(\mathtt{cechCofaceLin}\) with action \(\mathtt{cechCoface\_apply}\).
1119	  Second, localising \(D^\bullet\) at a spanning element \(s_r\) reproduces the
1120	  \emph{localised} {\v C}ech complex: the localised differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1121	  (action \(\mathtt{locDiff\_apply}\)) agrees, via
1122	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\), with the dependent-coefficient combinatorial

1126	  (\(\mathtt{fLoc\_isLocalizedModule}\)) intertwining \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) with
1127	  \(\mathtt{locDiff}\) (\(\mathtt{locDiff\_fLoc}\), \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\)).

1139	\begin{lemma}
1140	
1141	\leanok
1142	[Positive-degree exactness of the section {\v C}ech module complex]
1143	  \label{lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dDiff_exact}
1145	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1146	  With the notation of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}, the
1147	  un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\) is exact in positive
1148	  degrees: for every \(p\),
1149	  \[
1150	    \operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(d^{p+1} : D^{p+1} \to D^{p+2},\;
1151	      d^{p+2} : D^{p+2} \to D^{p+3}\bigr).
1152	  \]
1153	  This is step~(a) of the affine vanishing: the node-by-node exactness of the
1154	  un-localised module complex, from which the homology vanishing of the section
1155	  {\v C}ech complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, step~(c)) is read off.
1156	\end{lemma}
1157	
1158	\begin{proof}
1159	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1160	  \leanok
1161	  Exactness of \(D^\bullet\) at each positive-degree node is a local-to-global
1162	  statement over the spanning family. By the local-to-global exactness criterion
1163	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\) (with the
1164	  spanning hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\)), it
1165	  suffices to verify \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p+1}, d^{p+2})\) after
1166	  localising at each spanning element \(s_r\). In the localisation at \(s_r\) the
1167	  comparison family \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\) identifies the localised
1168	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) with the localised Čech differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1169	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} (using the localisation
1170	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\) of
1171	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\)). Exactness of that
1172	  localised differential is \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\), which is in turn the
1173	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial exactness transported along
1174	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\); the contracting homotopy at the fixed index
1175	  \(r\) (where \(s_r\) is invertible) furnishes \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\),
1176	  giving the localised exactness one localisation at a time. Reassembling over the
1177	  spanning family yields the un-localised exactness.
1178	\end{proof}

1354	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1355	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1356	
1357	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1358	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1359	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1360	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1361	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1362	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1363	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1364	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1365	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1366	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1367	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1368	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1369	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1370	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1371	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1372	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1373	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1374	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1375	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1376	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1377	\(\delta\)-functor identification.

1422	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1423	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1424	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1425	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1426	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1427	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1428	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1429	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1430	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1431	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1432	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1433	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1434	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1435	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1436	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1437	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1438	  \[
1439	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1440	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1441	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1442	  \]
1443	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1444	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1445	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1446	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1447	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1448	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1449	  \[
1450	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1451	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1452	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1453	  \]
1454	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1455	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1456	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1457	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1458	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1459	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1460	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1461	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1462	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1463	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1464	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1465	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1466	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1467	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1468	
1469	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1470	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1471	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1472	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1473	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1474	
1475	  The parallel \texttt{...Fam} declarations pinned above are the cover-agnostic

1477	  family \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) of opens carrying \emph{no} covering hypothesis — and
1478	  feed the family form of the {\v C}ech acyclicity lemma
1479	  (\(\operatorname{injective\_cech\_acyclicFam}\)).
1480	\end{definition}

1515	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1516	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1517	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1518	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1519	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,

1523	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),

1526	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1527	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1528	  \[
1529	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1530	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1531	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1532	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1533	  \]

1535	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1536	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1537	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1538	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1539	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1540	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1541	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1542	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.

1618	\begin{proof}
1619	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1620	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1621	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1622	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1623	  \[
1624	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1625	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1626	    \bigr)
1627	    \;\cong\;
1628	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1629	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1630	    \;\cong\;
1631	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1632	  \]
1633	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1634	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1635	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1636	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1637	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1638	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1639	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1640	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1641	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1642	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1643	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1644	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1645	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1646	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1647	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1648	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1649	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1650	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1651	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1652	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.

1686	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1687	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :

1693	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)

1728	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1729	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1730	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1731	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1732	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1733	  homology) and jointly conservative: a morphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is an isomorphism

1740	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on

1742	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1743	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1744	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1745	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of

1748	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1749	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1750	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1751	  quasi-isomorphism.

7568	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category with pullbacks, \(S \in \mathcal{C}\), and
7569	  \((Z_k \to S)_{k \in \mathrm{Fin}(n)}\) a finite family over \(S\). Then the wide pullback over
7570	  \(S\),
7571	  \[
7572	    \operatorname{widePullback} S\;(\lambda\,k.\ Z_k)\;(\lambda\,k.\ Z_k \to S),
7573	  \]
7574	  is the \(n\)-fold product \(\prod_{k} Z_k\) of the same family taken in the slice category
7575	  \(\operatorname{Over} S\) --- i.e.\ the \(n\)-fold fibre power over \(S\) is the \(n\)-fold product
7576	  in \(\operatorname{Over} S\). In particular the fibre power has the clean recursion
7577	  \(\prod_{k : \mathrm{Fin}(p+2)} Z_k \cong Z_0 \times_S \prod_{k : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1)} Z_{k+1}\)
7578	  along \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+2) \cong \mathrm{Fin}(1) \oplus \mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\).

7583	  terminal by Lemma~\ref{lem:over_mkIdTerminal_mathlib}, and the legs of the wide-pullback diagram
7584	  all land in this terminal base. By Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullbackCone_isLimitOfFan_mathlib} a
7585	  limiting fan of the legs is then a limit of the wide-pullback diagram, so the wide pullback over
7586	  \(S\) coincides with the product of the legs computed in \(\operatorname{Over} S\). The recursion
7587	  is the standard splitting of a finite product along \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+2) \cong \mathrm{Fin}(1)
7588	  \oplus \mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\) (a binary product with the head factor).

7659	  \uses{lem:prod_coproduct_distrib, lem:overProdLeftIsoPullback_mathlib}
7660	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a finitary pre-extensive category with pullbacks and \(S \in \mathcal{C}\).
7661	  For a finite index type \(\iota : \operatorname{Type} 0\), a family \((A_i)_{i \in \iota}\) of
7662	  objects of \(\operatorname{Over} S\), and a single \(B \in \operatorname{Over} S\), the
7663	  \emph{binary product in the slice} distributes over the coproduct:
7664	  \[
7665	    \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_{i} A_i\bigr) \times B
7666	      \;\cong\;
7667	    \coprod_{i} \bigl(A_i \times B\bigr)
7668	      \qquad\text{in } \operatorname{Over} S,
7669	  \]
7670	  where \(\times\) is the categorical binary product of \(\operatorname{Over} S\). This is the form in
7671	  which the inductive step of Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower} consumes distributivity:
7672	  the recursion lives entirely in \(\operatorname{Over} S\), so it needs the slice binary product, not
7673	  the \(\mathcal{C}\)-level fibre product of Lemma~\ref{lem:prod_coproduct_distrib}.
7674	\end{lemma}
7675	\begin{proof}
7676	  \uses{lem:prod_coproduct_distrib, lem:overProdLeftIsoPullback_mathlib}
7677	  The forgetful functor \(\operatorname{Over} S \to \mathcal{C}\) reflects isomorphisms: a morphism of
7678	  the slice is an isomorphism iff its underlying \(\mathcal{C}\)-morphism (its \(.\mathrm{left}\)) is.
7679	  It therefore suffices to identify the underlying objects. The coproduct in \(\operatorname{Over} S\)
7680	  is computed on underlying objects, \((\coprod_i A_i).\mathrm{left} = \coprod_i A_i.\mathrm{left}\),
7681	  and the underlying object of a binary product in \(\operatorname{Over} S\) is the fibre product of
7682	  the two structure maps, \((Y \times Z).\mathrm{left} \cong \operatorname{pullback} Y.\mathrm{hom}\,
7683	  Z.\mathrm{hom}\), via \(\operatorname{Over.prodLeftIsoPullback}\)
7684	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:overProdLeftIsoPullback_mathlib}). Under these identifications both sides become
7685	  the \(\mathcal{C}\)-level objects of the one-sided distributivity
7686	  Lemma~\ref{lem:prod_coproduct_distrib}: \((\coprod_i A_i.\mathrm{left}) \times_S B.\mathrm{left}\)
7687	  on the left and \(\coprod_i (A_i.\mathrm{left} \times_S B.\mathrm{left})\) on the right. Matching
7688	  \(.\mathrm{left}\) with that \(\mathcal{C}\)-level isomorphism, and noting all comparison maps commute
7689	  with the projection to \(S\), gives the slice isomorphism.
7690	\end{proof}

7692	\begin{lemma}
7693	[One-sided distributivity in \(\operatorname{Over} S\), product-on-the-right form]
7694	  \label{lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib_right}
7695	  \lean{CategoryTheory.FinitaryPreExtensive.overProd_coproduct_distrib_right}
7696	  \uses{lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib}
7697	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a finitary pre-extensive category with pullbacks and \(S \in \mathcal{C}\).
7698	  For a finite index type \(\iota : \operatorname{Type} 0\), a single \(A \in \operatorname{Over} S\)
7699	  and a family \((Y_i)_{i \in \iota}\) of objects of \(\operatorname{Over} S\), the slice binary
7700	  product distributes over the coproduct in the second argument:
7701	  \[
7702	    A \times \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_{i} Y_i\bigr)
7703	      \;\cong\;
7704	    \coprod_{i} \bigl(A \times Y_i\bigr)
7705	      \qquad\text{in } \operatorname{Over} S.
7706	  \]
7707	  This is the braiding-twin of Lemma~\ref{lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib}; the inductive step of
7708	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower} uses it to distribute the head factor over the
7709	  coproduct of multi-indexed fibre powers.
7710	\end{lemma}
7711	\begin{proof}
7712	  \uses{lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib}
7713	  Braid the slice binary product, \(A \times (\coprod_i Y_i) \cong (\coprod_i Y_i) \times A\), apply
7714	  the left-handed distributivity (Lemma~\ref{lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib}) to get
7715	  \(\coprod_i (Y_i \times A)\), and braid each summand back to \(A \times Y_i\). All three steps are
7716	  isomorphisms of \(\operatorname{Over} S\), so the composite is the asserted distributivity.
7717	\end{proof}

7731	
7732	\leanok
7733	[Nested coproduct flattens and reindexes to the next fibre power]
7734	  \label{lem:coproduct_fibrePower_reindex}
7735	  \lean{CategoryTheory.FinitaryPreExtensive.coproduct_fibrePower_reindex}
7736	  \uses{lem:sigmaSigmaIso_mathlib}
7737	  Let \(\iota\) be a finite index type, \(S \in \mathcal{C}\), \((X_i \to S)_i\) a family over
7738	  \(S\), and for \(\tau : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\to\iota\) write \(Y_\tau = X_{\tau(0)} \times_S \cdots
7739	  \times_S X_{\tau(p)}\). Then the nested coproduct flattens and reindexes to a coproduct over
7740	  \((p+2)\)-fold multi-indices:
7741	  \[
7742	    \coprod_{i}\coprod_{\tau} \bigl(X_i \times_S Y_\tau\bigr)
7743	      \;\cong\;
7744	    \coprod_{(i,\tau)} \bigl(X_i \times_S Y_\tau\bigr)
7745	      \;\cong\;
7746	    \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+2) \to \iota}
7747	      \bigl(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\bigr),
7748	  \]
7749	  where the second isomorphism is the reindexing \((i, \tau) \mapsto \sigma =
7750	  \operatorname{Fin.cons} i\,\tau\), a bijection \(\iota \times (\mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\to\iota) \cong
7751	  (\mathrm{Fin}(p+2)\to\iota)\), under which \(X_i \times_S Y_\tau\) is exactly the \((p+2)\)-fold
7752	  fibre power \(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\).
7753	\end{lemma}
7754	\begin{proof}
7755	  \uses{lem:sigmaSigmaIso_mathlib}
7756	  The first isomorphism is \(\operatorname{sigmaSigmaIso}\) of
7757	  Lemma~\ref{lem:sigmaSigmaIso_mathlib}, collapsing the doubly-indexed coproduct
7758	  \(\coprod_i \coprod_\tau\) into a coproduct over pairs \((i,\tau)\). The
7759	  \(\operatorname{Fin.cons}\) bijection \(\iota \times (\mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\to\iota) \cong
7760	  (\mathrm{Fin}(p+2)\to\iota)\), \((i,\tau)\mapsto \operatorname{Fin.cons} i\,\tau\), reindexes that
7761	  coproduct over \(\sigma\); and since \(\sigma(0) = i\) and \(\sigma(k+1) = \tau(k)\), the component
7762	  \(X_i \times_S Y_\tau = X_i \times_S (X_{\tau(0)} \times_S \cdots)\) is term-for-term the
7763	  \((p+2)\)-fold fibre power \(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\).
7764	\end{proof}

7766	\begin{lemma}

7773	    lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib, lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib_right,
7774	    lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}
7775	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category with pullbacks that is finitary pre-extensive
7776	  (\(\operatorname{FinitaryPreExtensive}\mathcal{C}\)), let \(\iota\) be a finite index type,

7778	  \(p\) the \((p+1)\)-fold wide fibre power of the coproduct map \(\coprod_i X_i \to S\) over \(S\)
7779	  decomposes as a coproduct over multi-indices:
7780	  \[
7781	    \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_{i} X_i\bigr)^{\times_S (p+1)}
7782	      \;\cong\;
7783	    \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
7784	      \bigl(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p)}\bigr),
7785	  \]
7786	  the \(\sigma\)-component being the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power of the legs \(X_{\sigma(k)} \to S\)
7787	  over \(S\), and the structure map of the coproduct being
7788	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\) of the component structure maps.
7789	
7790	  \emph{Slice-product normal form.} The proof carries out the recursion in
7791	  \(\operatorname{Over} S\), so the \(\sigma\)-component is presented as the iterated \emph{slice
7792	  product} \(\prod^{\mathrm{c}}_{k}\operatorname{Over.mk}\bigl(f(\sigma k)\bigr)\) over
7793	  \(\operatorname{Over} S\) rather than as the wide fibre power directly; the two are identified by
7794	  Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod} (wide fibre power over \(S\) equals the iterated product in
7795	  \(\operatorname{Over} S\)). The result is stated
7796	  abstractly for \(\operatorname{FinitaryPreExtensive}\mathcal{C}\) (reusable)
7797	  and then instantiated at \(\mathcal{C} = \operatorname{Scheme}\), which is finitary extensive by
7798	  Lemma~\ref{lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}.
7799	
7800	  \emph{Universe constraint.} This statement requires the index type \(\iota : \operatorname{Type} 0\):
7801	  the binary-distributivity primitive it rests on (Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_sigmaDesc_fst_mathlib}) does not
7802	  hold for index types in higher universes. When the cover index is finite but lives in a higher
7803	  universe, one first reindexes along an equivalence to \(\mathrm{Fin}(n)\) before applying this
7804	  result; see Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma}.
7805	\end{lemma}

7807	  \uses{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod, lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower_zero,
7808	    lem:prod_coproduct_distrib, lem:coproduct_fibrePower_reindex, lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib,
7809	    lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib_right, lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}
7810	  Induction on \(p\). Throughout, Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod} lets us treat the
7811	  \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power over \(S\) as the \((p+1)\)-fold product in \(\operatorname{Over} S\),
7812	  so the induction has the clean recursion \(P^{\times_S (p+2)} \cong P \times_S P^{\times_S (p+1)}\)
7813	  with \(P = \coprod_i X_i\).
7814	
7815	  \emph{Base step} \(p = 0\) is Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower_zero}: the \(1\)-fold
7816	  fibre power is \(\coprod_i X_i\) itself, indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(1) \to \iota\).
7817	
7818	  \emph{Inductive step}: assume \(\bigl(\coprod_i X_i\bigr)^{\times_S (p+1)} \cong \coprod_{\tau :
7819	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota} Y_\tau\) with \(Y_\tau = X_{\tau(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S
7820	  X_{\tau(p)}\). By the recursion of Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod} and the inductive
7821	  hypothesis,
7822	  \[
7823	    \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_i X_i\bigr)^{\times_S (p+2)}
7824	      \;\cong\;
7825	    \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_i X_i\bigr) \times_S \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_\tau Y_\tau\bigr).
7826	  \]
7827	  Applying the one-sided distributivity of
7828	  Lemma~\ref{lem:prod_coproduct_distrib} on each side turns the right-hand side into
7829	  \(\coprod_{i}\coprod_{\tau} \bigl(X_i \times_S Y_\tau\bigr)\). Since the recursion lives in
7830	  \(\operatorname{Over} S\), this distributivity is consumed in its \(\operatorname{Over} S\)
7831	  binary-product form (Lemma~\ref{lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib}), which expresses
7832	  \((\coprod_\tau Y_\tau) \times B \cong \coprod_\tau (Y_\tau \times B)\) for the binary product in the
7833	  slice rather than the \(\mathcal{C}\)-level pullback. Finally
7834	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_fibrePower_reindex} flattens this nested coproduct
7835	  (via \(\operatorname{sigmaSigmaIso}\)) and reindexes the pairs \((i,\tau)\) as
7836	  \(\sigma = \operatorname{Fin.cons} i\,\tau : \mathrm{Fin}(p+2) \to \iota\), identifying \(X_i
7837	  \times_S Y_\tau\) with \(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\). The structure
7838	  maps to \(S\) agree because each constituent isomorphism is built from \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\)
7839	  / product comparisons that commute with the projection to \(S\) by construction. Instantiating at
7840	  \(\mathcal{C} = \operatorname{Scheme}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}) gives the
7841	  scheme-level statement.

7846	[The wide fibre power of open immersions is the intersection open]
7847	  \label{lem:widePullback_openImm_inter}

7849	    AlgebraicGeometry.mem_iInf_opens_of_finite}
7850	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, lem:isPullback_opens_inf_mathlib}
7851	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an open cover and
7852	  \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I\) a multi-index. The \((p+1)\)-fold wide fibre power over
7853	  \(X\) of the open immersions \((U_{\sigma(k)} \hookrightarrow X)_{k}\) is the intersection open:
7854	  \[
7855	    \operatorname{widePullback} X \;(\lambda\,k.\ U_{\sigma(k)})\;(\lambda\,k.\ (U_{\sigma(k)}).\iota)
7856	      \;\cong\;
7857	    (\operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma).\mathrm{toScheme}
7858	      \;=\; \bigl(\textstyle\bigcap_k U_{\sigma(k)}\bigr).\mathrm{toScheme},
7859	  \]
7860	  over \(X\), and the structure map is the open immersion
7861	  \(j_\sigma : \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma \hookrightarrow X\) of
7862	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}.

7867	  immersions \(U.\iota, V.\iota\) is the intersection open \((U \sqcap V).\mathrm{toScheme}\) with
7868	  the two \(\operatorname{homOfLE}\) inclusions --- this is
7869	  \(\operatorname{isPullback\_opens\_inf}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:isPullback_opens_inf_mathlib}. The
7870	  wide pullback of the family \((U_{\sigma(k)})_{k}\) is the iterated binary pullback over \(X\)
7871	  along \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\), so iterating the binary intersection gives
7872	  \(\bigcap_k U_{\sigma(k)}\), which is the indexed infimum
7873	  \(\operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma = \bigcap_k \operatorname{coverOpen}
7874	  \mathcal{U}\,(\sigma(k))\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} (recall
7875	  \(\operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i = (\mathcal{U}.f\,i).\operatorname{opensRange}\) is the
7876	  image open \(U_i\)). The structure map to \(X\) is the inclusion of the intersection, namely
7877	  \(j_\sigma\).
7878	\end{proof}

7811	  \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power over \(S\) as the \((p+1)\)-fold product in \(\operatorname{Over} S\),
7812	  so the induction has the clean recursion \(P^{\times_S (p+2)} \cong P \times_S P^{\times_S (p+1)}\)
7813	  with \(P = \coprod_i X_i\).

7816	  fibre power is \(\coprod_i X_i\) itself, indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(1) \to \iota\).
7817	
7818	  \emph{Inductive step}: assume \(\bigl(\coprod_i X_i\bigr)^{\times_S (p+1)} \cong \coprod_{\tau :
7819	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota} Y_\tau\) with \(Y_\tau = X_{\tau(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S

7821	  hypothesis,

7830	  \(\operatorname{Over} S\), this distributivity is consumed in its \(\operatorname{Over} S\)
7831	  binary-product form (Lemma~\ref{lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib}), which expresses
7832	  \((\coprod_\tau Y_\tau) \times B \cong \coprod_\tau (Y_\tau \times B)\) for the binary product in the
7833	  slice rather than the \(\mathcal{C}\)-level pullback. Finally
7834	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_fibrePower_reindex} flattens this nested coproduct
7835	  (via \(\operatorname{sigmaSigmaIso}\)) and reindexes the pairs \((i,\tau)\) as
7836	  \(\sigma = \operatorname{Fin.cons} i\,\tau : \mathrm{Fin}(p+2) \to \iota\), identifying \(X_i
7837	  \times_S Y_\tau\) with \(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\). The structure
7838	  maps to \(S\) agree because each constituent isomorphism is built from \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\)
7839	  / product comparisons that commute with the projection to \(S\) by construction. Instantiating at
7840	  \(\mathcal{C} = \operatorname{Scheme}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}) gives the
7841	  scheme-level statement.

7897	    U_\sigma \;=\; \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma \;=\;
7898	      \bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)},

7904	  of the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\) is the intersection open, and the disjoint
7905	  union over \(\sigma\) assembles the {\v C}ech backbone.

7909	    lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod, lem:widePullback_openImm_inter}
7910	  Assemble the chain in \(\operatorname{Over} X\). By
7911	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cechBackbone_obj_widePullback} the degree-\(p\) object \(Y_p\) is the wide
7912	  pullback \(\bigl(\coprod_i U_i\bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}\), the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power of
7913	  \(q\) over \(X\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower} (instantiated at
7914	  \(\mathcal{C} = \operatorname{Scheme}\), with \(S = X\) and the family
7915	  \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)) this fibre power is the coproduct
7916	  \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I} \bigl(U_{\sigma(0)} \times_X \cdots \times_X
7917	  U_{\sigma(p)}\bigr)\) over the multi-indices. This decomposition delivers each \(\sigma\)-component in
7918	  its slice-product normal form, namely the iterated product
7919	  \(\prod^{\mathrm{c}}_{k}\operatorname{Over.mk}(U_{\sigma(k)} \hookrightarrow X)\) in
7920	  \(\operatorname{Over} X\); before it can be identified as an intersection open it must be converted
7921	  back to the wide-pullback form, which is exactly Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod} (the
7922	  iterated product over \(X\) in \(\operatorname{Over} X\) equals the wide fibre power over \(X\)). With
7923	  the component thus in wide-pullback form, Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullback_openImm_inter} applies: each
7924	  \(\sigma\)-component, being the wide fibre power over \(X\) of the open immersions
7925	  \(U_{\sigma(k)} \hookrightarrow X\), is the intersection open
7926	  \(U_\sigma = \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma = \bigcap_k U_{\sigma(k)}\), with
7927	  structure map the open immersion \(j_\sigma\). Composing these
7928	  isomorphisms gives \(Y_p \cong \coprod_\sigma U_\sigma\); the structure-map agreement is
7929	  automatic, since each step's isomorphism commutes with the projection to \(X\), so the
7930	  universal map out of the coproduct is \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(\sigma \mapsto j_\sigma)\).

7932	  \emph{Universe reduction.} The distributivity Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower} and the
7933	  binary-distributivity leaf it rests on (Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_sigmaDesc_fst_mathlib}) require the
7934	  index type to lie in \(\operatorname{Type} 0\); the cover index \(I\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) is a

7937	  \(\operatorname{Finite.equivFin}\), or \(\operatorname{Fintype.equivFin}\) once a \(\operatorname{Fintype}\)
7938	  instance is fixed), and reindex the cover family \((U_i \hookrightarrow X)\) and every coproduct- and

7940	  transports are involved. On the left, the source coproduct is reindexed along \(e\),
7941	  \[

7943	      \;\cong\;
7944	    \coprod_{j \in \mathrm{Fin}(n)} \bigl(\mathcal{U}.X \circ e^{-1}\bigr),
7945	  \]
7946	  the coproduct comparison induced by the index equivalence (\(\operatorname{Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\)).
7947	  On the right, the multi-index type is reindexed on its codomain,
7948	  \[
7949	    \bigl(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathrm{Fin}(n)\bigr)
7950	      \;\cong\;
7951	    \bigl(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I\bigr),
7952	  \]
7953	  the arrow-congruence \(\operatorname{Equiv.arrowCongr}\) applied to \(e^{-1}\) in the codomain, under
7954	  which the coproduct over \(\sigma\)-components of the \(\mathrm{Fin}(n)\)-decomposition matches the
7955	  coproduct over the original multi-indices. Apply the \(\operatorname{Type} 0\) decomposition of
7956	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower} at \(\mathrm{Fin}(n)\), then transport the resulting
7957	  isomorphism back along these two equivalences. Reindexing a coproduct (resp.\ a wide fibre power)
7958	  along a bijection of index types is an isomorphism compatible with the structure maps, so the
7959	  transported isomorphism is again \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(\sigma \mapsto j_\sigma)\) over \(X\).
7960	\end{proof}

9524	  \(A = j_!\mathcal{O}_V\). It suffices, by full faithfulness of \(\operatorname{coyoneda}\)
9525	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:coyoneda_fullyFaithful_mathlib}, which reflects isomorphisms), to produce a natural
9526	  isomorphism of the corepresented functors

9532	  \begin{enumerate}
9533	    \item The equivalence–coyoneda isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:compCoyonedaIso_mathlib}, applied to the
9534	      adjunction underlying \(\Phi\), gives

9581	  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi\) the induced equivalence of module categories,
9582	  and \(V \subseteq X\) an open with image \(V' = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,V \subseteq Y\). The
9583	  homeomorphism underlying \(\varphi\) restricts to a homeomorphism \(V \cong V'\), hence to an
9584	  equivalence of the opens-sites \(\operatorname{Opens} V \simeq \operatorname{Opens} V'\); let
9585	  \[
9586	    e : (X.\mathrm{Modules} \!\restriction\! V) \;\simeq\; (Y.\mathrm{Modules} \!\restriction\! V')
9587	  \]
9588	  be the induced equivalence of restricted-module categories obtained from the site-equivalence
9589	  transport of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}. Then for every
9590	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(H\) there is a comparison isomorphism
9591	  \[
9592	    e.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(H.\operatorname{over} V\bigr)
9593	      \;\cong\;
9594	    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over} V',
9595	  \]
9596	  identifying ``restrict to \(V\) then push forward over \(V'\)'' with ``push forward then restrict to
9597	  \(V'\)''.

9599	\begin{proof}
9600	  \uses{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}
9601	  Both sides are computed open-by-open on the subspace site of \(V'\). For an open
9602	  \(W \subseteq V'\) with preimage \(W_0 = \varphi.\mathrm{hom}^{-1}\,W \subseteq V\), the sections of the
9603	  right-hand restriction are the sections of \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) over the image
9604	  open \(W\), which — pushforward acting on opens by the inverse-image homeomorphism — are the sections
9605	  of \(H\) over \(W_0\) (the restriction-of-objects identity of Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib},
9606	  applied to the open immersions \(V \hookrightarrow X\) and \(V' \hookrightarrow Y\)). The same
9607	  sections compute the left-hand side: the site-equivalence transport
9608	  \(e\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib} relabels the open \(W\) of \(V'\)
9609	  to the corresponding open \(W_0\) of \(V\) and reads off \(H.\operatorname{over} V\) there. The two
9610	  presheaves of sections therefore agree, compatibly with restriction maps, and the comparison is a
9611	  morphism of sheaves of modules over \(V'\); being an isomorphism on every section group it is an
9612	  isomorphism. This is the open-restriction analogue of the \(\operatorname{bind}\) construction's
9613	  per-piece transport, with the homeomorphism-induced opens-site equivalence in place of the
9614	  iterated-slice equivalence.

9647	    lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib,
9648	    lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
9649	  Quasi-coherence is a \emph{local} datum: it transports across the equivalence \(\Phi\) by moving
9650	  \(H\)'s local presentations to the image cover, member by member. We never seek a global presentation
9651	  of \(H\) (it has none — \(H\) is quasi-coherent only on the abstract source); we move the local
9652	  presentations one open at a time.
9653	
9654	  \emph{The cover.} Extract \(H\)'s quasi-coherence datum
9655	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a family \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) of opens covering
9656	  \(X\) together with, for each \(i\), a presentation \(p_i\) of \(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\). Form the
9657	  image cover \(V_i = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,U_i\) of \(Y\); it covers \(Y\) because the underlying
9658	  map of \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}\) is a homeomorphism, so the preimages of a cover form a cover (the image
9659	  of an affine open under the scheme isomorphism is again affine,
9660	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}, when one wants the cover to remain affine). By
9661	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib} it suffices to show that
9662	  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\) is quasi-coherent for every \(i\).
9663	
9664	  \emph{Per-member transport.} Fix \(i\) and let
9665	  \(e_i : (X.\mathrm{Modules}\!\restriction\! U_i) \simeq (Y.\mathrm{Modules}\!\restriction\! V_i)\) be
9666	  the opens-site equivalence induced by \(\varphi\) restricted to \(U_i\), built from
9667	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}. This \(e_i\) preserves colimits and carries
9668	  the structure-sheaf unit to the structure-sheaf unit (it is a site equivalence whose underlying base
9669	  is a homeomorphism), so by Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib} the presentation \(p_i\) of
9670	  \(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\) transports to a presentation of
9671	  \(e_i.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}(H.\operatorname{over} U_i)\). The comparison isomorphism of
9672	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_commutes_restriction},
9673	  \[
9674	    e_i.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\bigr)
9675	      \;\cong\;
9676	    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over} V_i,
9677	  \]
9678	  then carries that presentation, via Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib}, to a presentation of
9679	  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\). A sheaf of modules with a (global)
9680	  presentation is quasi-coherent, so each member of the image cover is quasi-coherent, and the claim
9681	  follows by Lemma~\ref{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib}.
9682	
9683	  This is the isomorphism case of the Stacks functoriality remark (``it is in general not the case that
9684	  the pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent''; here it holds because \(\varphi\) is an
9685	  isomorphism, equivalently \(\varphi_* = (\varphi^{-1})^*\) is a pullback).
9686	\end{proof}
9687	
9688	\begin{lemma}[The image of an affine open under an open immersion is affine]
9689	  \label{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
9690	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.isAffineOpen_iff_of_isOpenImmersion}
9691	  \mathlibok
9692	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
9693	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an open immersion of schemes (in particular an isomorphism) and \(U\) an open
9694	  of \(X\). Then the image open \(f \,{''}\, U\) is affine if and only if \(U\) is affine. In
9695	  particular the image \(V'\) of an affine open \(V\) under a scheme isomorphism is an affine open.
9696	\end{lemma}
9697	
9698	\begin{lemma}
9699	[Ext transport along the whole-scheme spectrum equivalence (Need~\#1)]
9700	  \label{lem:modules_isoSpec_ext_transport}
9701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.modulesIsoSpecExtTransport,
9702	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pushforwardEquivOfIso,
9703	    AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardEquivOfIso_functor_additive,
9704	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pushforwardExtAddEquiv}
9705	  \uses{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib, lem:ext_mapExactFunctor_mathlib,
9706	    lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib, lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso,
9707	    lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh, lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
9708	  Let \(U\) be a scheme with \([\,\mathrm{IsAffine}\ U\,]\), let \(V \subseteq U\) be an affine open,
9709	  and let \(\mathcal{H}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. The whole-scheme isomorphism
9710	  \(U \cong \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib})
9711	  induces, for every \(q\), an isomorphism
9712	  \[
9713	    \operatorname{Ext}^q_{U}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_V,\, \mathcal{H}\bigr)
9714	      \;\cong\;
9715	    \operatorname{Ext}^q_{\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'},\, \mathcal{H}'\bigr),
9716	  \]
9717	  where \(V'\) is the affine open image of \(V\) under the isomorphism and \(\mathcal{H}'\) is the
9718	  transported (still quasi-coherent) coefficient. In particular the residual over \(U\) vanishes iff
9719	  its transport over \(\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U\) does.
9720	\end{lemma}
9721	\begin{proof}
9722	  \uses{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib, lem:ext_mapExactFunctor_mathlib,
9723	    lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib, lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso,
9724	    lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh, lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
9725	  Since \(U\) is affine, \(\operatorname{Scheme.isoSpec}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib})
9726	  gives a genuine isomorphism of schemes \(\varphi : U \cong \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)\).
9727	  Assemble from the pushforward functors and their coherences
9728	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib}) the equivalence of module categories
9729	  \[
9730	    \Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi :
9731	      U.\mathrm{Modules} \;\simeq\; (\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U).\mathrm{Modules},
9732	  \]
9733	  with \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}\) the pushforward along \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\) and its quasi-inverse the
9734	  pushforward along \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}\); the unit and counit are supplied by the composition and
9735	  identity coherences. An equivalence is exact and additive, so
9736	  \(\operatorname{Ext.mapExactFunctor}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_mapExactFunctor_mathlib}) applies and
9737	  yields a map on \(\operatorname{Ext}\); since \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}\), being one half of an
9738	  equivalence, preserves injective objects, its induced \(\operatorname{Ext}\)-map is bijective
9739	  (using enough injectives on \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\)), giving an additive isomorphism. This gives
9740	  the displayed isomorphism once the two arguments are identified. For the first
9741	  argument, a scheme isomorphism carries the corepresenting object along
9742	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso}): \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V) \cong
9743	  j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'}\), where \(V' = \varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V\) is the image, an affine open of
9744	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}. For the second,
9745	  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(\mathcal{H}) = \mathcal{H}'\) is again quasi-coherent because pushforward along
9746	  an isomorphism preserves quasi-coherence (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}).
9747	\end{proof}
9748	
9749	\begin{lemma}
9750	[Serre Ext-vanishing over an affine scheme via the spectrum transport]
9751	  \label{lem:ext_jShriekOU_eq_zero_of_specIso}
9752	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ext_jShriekOU_eq_zero_of_specIso}
9753	  \uses{lem:modules_isoSpec_ext_transport, lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso,
9754	    lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh, lem:affine_serre_vanishing_general_open,
9755	    lem:enoughInjectives_of_hasInjectiveResolutions, lem:subsingleton_ext_of_iso_fst}
9756	  Let \(U\) be a scheme with chosen injective resolutions on \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\), let
9757	  \(\varphi : U \cong \operatorname{Spec} R\) be an isomorphism of schemes,
9758	  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi\) the induced equivalence of module categories,
9759	  \(V \subseteq U\) an open and \(\mathcal{H}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. Suppose
9760	  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V) \cong j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'}\) for some \emph{affine} open
9761	  \(V' \subseteq \operatorname{Spec} R\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso}) and that
9762	  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(\mathcal{H})\) is quasi-coherent
9763	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}). Then for every \(q \geq 1\) every class
9764	  \(e \in \operatorname{Ext}^q_U(j_!\mathcal{O}_V, \mathcal{H})\) vanishes.
9765	\end{lemma}
9766	\begin{proof}
9767	  \uses{lem:modules_isoSpec_ext_transport, lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso,
9768	    lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh, lem:affine_serre_vanishing_general_open,
9769	    lem:enoughInjectives_of_hasInjectiveResolutions, lem:subsingleton_ext_of_iso_fst}
9770	  The chosen injective resolutions provide enough injectives on \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\)
9771	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:enoughInjectives_of_hasInjectiveResolutions}), which transport across the equivalence
9772	  \(\Phi\) to enough injectives on \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Modules}\). Over
9773	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) the general-affine-open Serre vanishing
9774	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing_general_open}), applied to the affine open \(V'\) and the
9775	  quasi-coherent coefficient \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(\mathcal{H})\), makes
9776	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'},\, \Phi.\mathrm{functor}\,\mathcal{H})\) a subsingleton.
9777	  Transferring along the transport isomorphism
9778	  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V) \cong j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'}\) in the first Ext-argument
9779	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:subsingleton_ext_of_iso_fst}) shows

9940	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
9941	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
9942	
9943	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
9944	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
9945	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
9946	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
9947	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
9948	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
9949	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
9950	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
9951	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
9952	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
9953	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
9954	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
9955	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
9956	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
9957	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
9958	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
9959	  role in the applications.
9960	\end{lemma}
9961	
9962	\begin{proof}
9963	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
9964	  Apply the abstract Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with the
9965	  right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
9966	  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
9967	  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
9968	  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. The two hypotheses of the
9969	  abstract lemma are exactly the geometric sub-lemmas established above:
9970	  \begin{enumerate}
9971	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
9972	      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
9973	      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
9974	      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
9975	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
9976	      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
9977	      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
9978	  \end{enumerate}
9979	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
9980	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
9981	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
9982	  resolution,
9983	  \[
9984	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
9985	      \;\cong\;
9986	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
9987	  \]
9988	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
9989	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
9990	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
9991	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
9992	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
9993	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
9994	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
9995	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
9996	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
9997	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
9998	  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
9999	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
10000	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).
10001	\end{proof}

55	### `cechAugmented_exact` — sections/sheafification route (P5a resolution input)
56	The augmented Čech complex is a resolution of `F` (ANY `O_X`-module, ANY open cover; qcoh/affineness
57	are needed only by the downstream f_*-acyclicity, not by this exactness). Route: reflect `IsZero(Hᵖ)`

59	helper — `reflects_exact_of_faithful` needs `[Abelian]`, avoid); the homology SHEAF = sheafification of
60	the presheaf homology (`homologyIsoSheafify`, built); the presheaf homology is **locally zero on the
61	basis `{V ⊆ some Uᵢ}`** because the restricted cover `{Uₛ∩V}` then has a member `=V`, giving the
62	cover-agnostic prepend-`i_fix` contracting homotopy (`CombinatorialCech.combHomotopy`/`combHomotopy_spec`,
63	F-valued objectwise analogue), so its sheafification vanishes via `sheafify_kills_locally_zero` + the
64	`toSheaf∘sheafify ≅ presheafToSheaf∘forget` square (`PresheafOfModules.sheafificationCompToSheaf`).
65	DEAD ends: stalk functor (no `SheafOfModules.stalk`); the tilde/standard-cover vanishing as the local

67	(CIRCULAR = `Ȟᵖ(V,·)`≠0). Analogist: `analogies/tosheaf-reflect.md`.

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
